serving.natural_language_portion: One plain bun typically weighs approximately 60 g and is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or light snack on its own or with a spread.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Plain Bun (F05030009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread / soft white roll analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'plain bun' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is estimated from the closest approved analogous food: white bread roll / soft white bread (GI ~71, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 71 × 36.89 × 0.60 / 100 ≈ 15.7 for a 60 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (≈17 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium at 229 mg/100 g with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit plain bun consumption due to its very high saturated fat content (≈17 g/100 g), which promotes arterial stiffness and cardiovascular risk, compounded by borderline sodium (229 mg/100 g) and negligible potassium or fibre to offset these effects; limit to one small bun per occasion and prefer wholemeal or lower-fat alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (≈71) from refined wheat flour with low dietary fibre (2.05 g/100 g) and very high fat (40.42 g/100 g) that may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid plain bun as a regular staple because its high GI (≈71), low fibre content (2.05 g/100 g), and very high fat (40.42 g/100 g) from shortening combine to produce a high glycaemic load and may worsen insulin resistance over time; choose wholemeal or high-fibre bread alternatives instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (≈17 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (≈0.5 g/100 g) from shortening, both strongly atherogenic
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid plain bun made with shortening or lard, as its very high saturated fat (≈17 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (≈0.5 g/100 g) significantly raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherosclerosis; select baked goods made with unsaturated oils and higher fibre content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (≈0.5 g/100 g) and very high saturated fat (≈17 g/100 g) are strongly pro-inflammatory; refined flour contributes to inflammatory glycaemic load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid plain bun made with shortening because even small amounts of trans fat (≈0.5 g/100 g) are strongly pro-inflammatory, and the very high saturated fat (≈17 g/100 g) further exacerbates systemic inflammation; check ingredient labels for 'partially hydrogenated vegetable oil' or 'shortening' and choose anti-inflammatory alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain bun is a simple, soft, round bread roll made from refined wheat flour, water, shortening or lard, sugar, salt, and yeast. It is baked until lightly golden and has a soft, pillowy crumb with a thin, tender crust. The flavour is mildly sweet and buttery, with no filling or toppings. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, plain buns are commonly eaten at breakfast, often alongside kaya (coconut jam) and butter, or consumed as a light snack. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records this food at 541 kcal/100 g, with a notably high fat content of 40.42 g/100 g — substantially higher than typical Western bread rolls — reflecting the use of shortening or lard in traditional local bakery formulations. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 17 g/100 g and trans fat at approximately 0.5 g/100 g from shortening. Carbohydrate is 36.89 g/100 g with only 2.05 g dietary fibre, yielding a high glycaemic index of approximately 71. Sodium is 229 mg/100 g. These characteristics make plain bun a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is not suitable as a regular dietary staple for these populations. Wholemeal or high-fibre bread alternatives are strongly preferred for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plain bun?
Is plain bun high in fat?
Is plain bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05030009) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved match (white bread roll, GI ≈ 71). Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from known shortening/lard composition consistent with HPB source context; these are flagged as estimates.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this plain bun entry is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05030009) as of 2026-04-16. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB source for this entry. trans_fat_g of 0.5 g/100 g is an estimate based on typical shortening content in local bakery plain buns; not directly measured in the HPB record. saturated_fat_g of 17.0 g/100 g is estimated from total fat composition consistent with shortening/lard use; not directly reported in HPB record. potassium_mg of 80 mg/100 g is an estimate from USDA SR Legacy analogous white bread roll entry used as a supplementary reference. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (white bread roll); no direct GI entry exists for this specific product. Halal status is not confirmed — traditional plain buns may use lard; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the manufacturer. All core macros (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
